explanations.1: Since like poles repel each other, the north pole of the compass needle will be repelled by the north pole of the bar magnet.
explanations.2: Magnetic poles always occur in pairs, so breaking a magnet results in two new magnets each having a full dipole.
explanations.3: In unmagnetized ferromagnetic materials, domains are internally aligned but randomly oriented relative to one another, resulting in zero net magnetization.
explanations.4: The Curie temperature is the specific point above which thermal motion is strong enough to randomize domain orientations.
explanations.5: Because magnetic monopoles do not exist, field lines do not begin or end on charges but must form continuous loops.
explanations.6: Magnetic field direction is defined by the orientation of a north-seeking pole in that field.
explanations.7: The Tesla is the standard SI unit, defined as 1 Newton per Ampere-meter.
explanations.8: RHR-1 identifies the force on a positive charge as being perpendicular to the palm of the hand.
explanations.9: Using $F = qvB \sin(90^\circ)$, $F = (20 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C})(10 \text{ m/s})(5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}) = 1 \times 10^{-11}$ N.
explanations.10: Because the force is always perpendicular to the displacement, work is zero and kinetic energy remains constant.
explanations.11: Using $r = mv/qB$, the radius is calculated as $(9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 6 \times 10^7) / (1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5) \approx 6.83 \times 10^{-4}$ m.
explanations.12: The perpendicular velocity component causes circular motion while the parallel component remains constant, resulting in a helix.
explanations.13: The sign of the Hall emf depends on the direction of the magnetic force on the specific type of charge carrier.
explanations.14: The Hall emf is calculated using $\varepsilon = Blv = (0.100 \text{ T})(0.004 \text{ m})(0.200 \text{ m/s}) = 8 \times 10^{-5}$ V.
explanations.15: Equilibrium is reached when the electric force $qE$ equals the magnetic force $qvB$, preventing further charge separation.
explanations.16: Since charges are confined within the wire, the force they experience is physically transmitted to the wire itself.
explanations.17: Using $F = IlB \sin(90^\circ)$, $F = (20.0 \text{ A})(0.0500 \text{ m})(1.50 \text{ T}) = 1.50$ N.
explanations.18: MHD utilizes the Lorentz force on a conducting fluid to create pressure and flow.
explanations.19: Torque is maximum when $\theta = 90^\circ$ (where $\theta$ is the angle between the field and the normal to the loop), which occurs when the loop's plane is parallel to the field.
explanations.20: $\tau = NIAB = (100)(15 \text{ A})(0.1 \text{ m} \times 0.1 \text{ m})(2.00 \text{ T}) = 30$ N$\cdot$m.
explanations.21: Without reversing the current, the loop would oscillate back and forth instead of completing a full rotation.
explanations.22: The formula $B = \mu_0 I / (2\pi r)$ shows that field strength is inversely proportional to the distance $r$.
explanations.23: RHR-2 relates the direction of current to the circular magnetic field lines that surround it.
explanations.24: Solenoids are designed to produce a strong, highly uniform magnetic field within their interior volume.
explanations.26: Currents in the same direction produce an attractive magnetic force, which is the basis for the pinch effect.
explanations.27: The Ampere is defined by the force between two parallel conductors separated by exactly 1 meter in a vacuum.
explanations.28: For the forces to balance ($qE = qvB$), the velocity must equal the ratio of the electric field to the magnetic field.
explanations.29: About two-thirds of the atoms in the human body are hydrogen, making it the most effective target for high-resolution imaging.
explanations.30: Since $r = mv/qB$ and $v$, $q$, and $B$ are constant, the radius $r$ depends linearly on the mass $m$.
